data.title: Tesla Is Trading Solely On Elon, Robotaxis Appear Set To Fail
data.description:
Elon Musk Is the Valuation

If you are here fully convinced Tesla, Inc.’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) valuation is so high because of how progressive and great this company is, you have experienced yourself a “Musk Premium.” In this article, I won’t be pretending Tesla trades on fundamentals because well, it doesn’t, and it never really has. It trades on CEO Elon Musk, on his, without a doubt, brilliant vision and beautiful stories about the future, and for years, it worked. Up until a few months ago, Musk was seen as a visionary and a genius, and Tesla's stock price reflected just that. But does this era come to an end just before our eyes?

Since Elon Musk began his “political career,” his character in the eyes of the public started to shift from an idol he once was to the devil, who has no idea what he is doing. Probably all of you remember that in early 2025, Musk was appointed head of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE). It was supposed to be the department that saved the government some money, but instead, it turned into chaos. Musk, as the head of DOGE, tried to push for controversial policies like eliminating USAID and the Department of Education, and caused almost 300,000 U.S. citizens to lose their jobs, mostly in governmental institutions. After only 130 days, he stepped down.

Elon Musk's political efforts caused Tesla stock to pull back in price significantly. Not surprisingly, at least for me, this time not only was the stock hurt, but also Tesla in the field, where people are supposed to buy cars. As a result, just a few months later, we can see that deliveries and revenues are dropping, while competitors, like BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), are confidently taking Tesla’s lost market share to themselves. Of course, Elon Musk still has the power to boost the stock price with buzzwords and promises, like robotaxis, AGI, Optimus, etc.

But with the latest Elon Musk-Donald Trump public pressure, if I were a Tesla investor, I would be seriously concerned. Tesla’s valuation is inextricably tied to Elon Musk, and if Elon Musk seriously annoys President Donald Trump, who is not the most easy-going person when it comes to ego-games and happens to be the most powerful man today in the U.S., the company could be hit immensely.

Right now, it looks like Musk is open to making peace (not that this is surprising, in this battle, I don’t think he stood a high chance), and Trump says everything’s “going very well.” There’s even a phone call scheduled. I am curious how this story goes because it’s one of the factors that could continue to hurt Tesla’s stock price, but fundamentally, it doesn’t change anything. And now I will explain why.

Valuation Metrics: The 900% Illusion

The stock appears overvalued immensely based on almost all of the metrics, like P/E, P/S, EV/EBITDA, etc. This is the Musk premium I mentioned at the beginning. Without a doubt, the company was one of a kind in 2019, and I get why people were so excited back then. Elon Musk is a brilliant man, and his vision and ideas have helped humanity make huge progress. But now, it’s starting to look like the edge we saw changed with regard to Elon Musk, and his focus has shifted from innovation. Besides, Tesla is not the same as it was five years ago. It appears there are many companies that can build the same products, some are cheaper, some are of better quality. So if the battle with the President doesn’t end well for Elon Musk, and there are several ways how Trump could hurt him, what will be left of Tesla?

Let’s break this down: Tesla’s forward P/E ratio is over 180, compared to a sector median of around 16. EV/EBITDA? Tesla is trading at 76x, versus an industry average closer to 9x. EV/Sales? Tesla sits at 11x, while traditional automakers trade closer to 1x. Hopefully, you can see for yourself that this is not just expensive, it’s disconnected from reality.

If you take Elon Musk out of the equation and the brand that many people still truly believe in, I believe this stock would trade at $20–25, and the company definitely wouldn’t be a trillion-dollar empire, it just was before the news hit the stock price. I am not saying this is a fair value for the stock price because, let’s be real, the brand is one of the strongest on the market, even after this reputational hit. But still, as far as Musk's bad reputation goes, the more Tesla's stock price will incline toward reality. It has a long way to go because the reality is that people do not want to buy Teslas after Musk's publicity in the office. And when I say people, I don't even mean in the U.S. My friends in Israel, where I live, in Ukraine, where I am from, and all over Europe, are selling their Teslas to not associate with this person.

Market Share Collapse: From Dominance to Decline

I am following Tesla’s news, and I read sometimes what my colleagues on Seeking Alpha have to say about this company, and this stock gets so much coverage that I don’t feel like I have a lot to add besides my own vision, perspective, and observations. In Israel, on roads in the past year, from a majority of electric vehicles being Teslas and a few Chinese brands here and there, we switched to a huge number of BYDs, with some other Chinese brands like XPeng (XPEV), and Tesla seriously losing this battle.

I actually can say that the area I live in is a higher-middle class area with mostly young families that are working in tech companies, there is a similar number of BYDs to some very popular brands here, like Mazda or Toyota. I also noticed that many tech companies give out BYD to their workers in leasing or as a work vehicle. I didn’t share this observation of mine before I saw Tesla sales in the UK and Europe because I thought it was some trend only here, in central Israel. But now we know that Tesla's sales were down 36% in the UK in May 2025 alone. Meanwhile, BYD outsold Tesla there.

But it’s not the only problem. Margins are also under pressure. Tesla as a company has the support of higher-margin segments like energy and storage, but still, their overall profitability is weakening. To get to the real margin, I looked at just the automotive sales revenue, which was $12.9 billion, not including leasing or anything else. Then I subtracted the cost of automotive sales, which is approximately $11.5 billion. That leaves a gross profit of about $1.46 billion and an automotive margin of 11.3%. By the way, if you take out $595 million in regulatory credits, you will get the margin closer to 8–9%. All of the numbers are from the operations statement from the Q1 presentation:

For context, two years ago, Tesla’s vehicle margin was about 25%. Calculate for yourself the drop in margins, but I got that it’s more than 50%. And yet, the stock still trades like margins are expanding and everything is fine. AI-powered Google search provided the information about the margins back in 2022 and 2023:

Besides, let’s not forget that in Q1 2025, Tesla’s automotive revenue dropped 20% year-over-year. Earnings per share fell by over 50%. Profit margins are the lowest they've been since 2019. And despite that, the stock trades at more than 10x sales, over 180x forward earnings, and nearly 77x forward EBITDA.

Robotaxi Fantasy vs Reality

I often hear that bulls refer to robotaxis as something that will change the company and bring prosperity. But will it? I don’t know if you saw the information that Alphabet's (GOOG) Waymo taxis are equipped much better than Tesla and that Tesla most likely won’t be able to launch robotaxis, at least not the ones that won’t be controlled by humans. Yes, yes, the only robotaxi possibility we are facing right now, it’s not even a robotaxi. Waymo is powered by the fifth-generation Waymo Driver and guided by five lidar sensors, six radars, and 29 cameras. Tesla simply upgraded its software and called it Robotaxi. It has only eight cameras.

Besides, Tesla's Full Self-Driving ("FSD") is Level 2, which means it requires constant driver supervision. And you have Waymo on the other hand, which has been at Level 4 for a long time already, and they are already working, people are using them and showing real results. Tesla’s promise of full autonomy has been delayed for years, and yet, the company hasn’t even reached Level 3 autonomy, which allows limited hands-off operation. Even if Tesla somehow reaches Level 3 soon and then immediately Level 4, what so big will happen? It’s not the first mover anymore in this space. I genuinely don’t understand why all of the buzz.

Also, Tesla quietly replaced the $25K Model 2 with a robotaxi promise. So instead of building something more affordable, which was actually also promised before, they went all in on something that doesn’t even have a timeline, let alone regulatory approval. This is another confirmation that the stock isn’t driven by deliveries, but by stories and narratives.

What's Tesla Worth Without Musk?

So, let’s ask the obvious: what is Tesla worth without the Musk premium? Based on current valuation metrics, Tesla is trading at about 900% above the sector median. That would imply a fair value of around $20–25 per share if it were some other average, not-so-hyped stock. Besides Musk, I don’t see why the company is priced so high, and of course, the stock can stay so expensive for a long time from now, because people like to fall in love with hyped stories and put their money in them. But I know for a fact that when this Musk bubble bursts, I won’t be the one to lose my money on it.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Thornburg Income & U.S. Government Bonds Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The first quarter of 2025 was characterized by significant volatility and complexity across global financial markets that was not driven by a singular trend but was a tug-of-war between multiple forces. Aggressive fiscal measures, particularly new tariffs, injected uncertainty and inflationary pressures, initially steepening the yield curve.

However, the Federal Reserve maintained a patient stance, signaling potential rate cuts later in the year amid concerns of slowing economic momentum. This dovish tilt capped longer-term yields and supported risk assets, including corporate credit, which saw modest spread tightening despite the macro headwinds. Inflation-linked bonds also garnered attention as tariff-induced price pressures became a focal point. The quarter concluded with markets closely watching inflation data and central bank rhetoric for clearer signals on the future path of rates and the ultimate impact of fiscal policy on the broader economy.

First Quarter 2025 Performance Highlights

  • In 1Q25, the Limited Term Income Portfolio (I share class) returned 2.27%, -15 basis points behind the Bloomberg Intermediate U.S. Government/Credit Index. Year to date, the portfolio (I share class) returned 2.27%, -15 basis points behind the index.
  • The portfolio maintained a duration roughly 0.28 years shorter than the index, which was problematic during a period of falling yields. As a result, duration was a detractor from relative performance, accounting for -11bps during the quarter.
  • The portfolio’s performance during the quarter was driven by its underweight allocation to Treasuries securities versus the Index which accounted for -65bps of relative underperformance.
  • The portfolio’s relative performance did benefit from its larger allocation to Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMO) and Mortgage Pass-Through (MPT) securities. The allocation to CMOs contributed 25bps to relative performance during the quarter while the MPT exposure contributed 26bps to relative performance.

Current Positioning and Outlook

Consistent with our mandate, we actively adjusted portfolio positioning throughout the quarter in response to evolving market conditions and relative value opportunities. We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors. While detailed shifts are part of our continuous management, our active management approach proved crucial in navigating the quarter's volatility.

The strategy remained positioned with a longer duration and higher quality bias compared to its long-term range, driven by attractive real yields and very tight credit spreads. However, the market rewarded the opposite. Security selection, driven by our bottom-up fundamental credit research, was a key contributor, allowing us to identify resilient issuers across various sectors.

We maintained broad diversification across fixed-income sectors with an emphasis on sectors demonstrating resilience or offering compelling risk/reward profiles identified through our research. The allocations to Treasuries, investment grade corporate credit, collateralized mortgage obligations, and mortgage pass-throughs were all large drivers of absolute returns during the period.

The portfolio continued to invest across the investment-grade credit spectrum, focused on fundamentally sound issuers offering attractive compensation for risk. This included selectively chosen corporate bonds with stronger balance sheets and more predictable cash flows which helped drive absolute performance during the quarter. Our primary approach remained identifying mispriced securities through rigorous bottom-up analysis, but opportunities were few and far between given spread levels.

Looking ahead, uncertainty surrounding inflation, central bank policy paths, economic growth trajectories, and geopolitical events, including trade policy, persists. We anticipate continued market volatility, which often creates opportunities for active managers. We remain committed to our disciplined investment process, combining top-down macroeconomic insights with intensive bottom-up credit research to identify relative value across the fixed income universe. Our focus continues to be on generating attractive income and risk-adjusted total returns for our clients over the long term.

data.title: REITs: Income Goldmine Or Value Pitfall?
data.description:
REITs are one of the fundamental cornerstones for most income-based strategies and portfolios. They embody the necessary characteristics that support durable current income, inflation protection and gradual price appreciation.

I like to label REITs as quasi-equity instruments. With a quasi-equity I mean a combination of bond-like and equity-like features.

The bond element comes through the following items:

  • Long-term lease agreements with relatively fixed prices (similar to coupons).
  • Relatively minor organic growth opportunities due to the requirement to distribute the lion's share of earnings in dividends.
  • Generally, protected asset base with quite low business risk.

The equity element kicks in through the following items:

  • Leases tied to annual fixed rate or CPI-linked escalators.
  • The ability to secure some growth by reinvesting the undistributed cash amount, or cash that is collected by selling properties at a higher price than what was initially invested.
  • The ability to issue fresh equity that could be combined with an additional leverage to expand the portfolio in an accretive fashion.

The bond factors render the valuations relatively low, which, in turn, help drive up the dividend yields.

The equity factors bring in the dividend inflation protection aspect as well as the potential for a gradual price appreciation return component.

Again, if we put these things together, I think you will agree with me that we arrive at an investment product that fits very well into most income based strategies/portfolios.

Having said that, there is a catch.

And the catch is that nobody wants to have high exposure to an asset base that systematically underperforms other income alternatives. And the situation becomes even worse if the dividends themselves do not match or exceed CPI (inflation) levels in the economy on the ground.

Since 2022, REITs could be put into that category.

Namely, since interest rates started to go up, the REITs followed, but just in the opposite direction.

Now, if we turn back to the quasi-equity story and zoom into the bond-like features, we will have to conclude that all of this makes sense. Bonds are high duration assets that are negatively correlated to the interest rates. The higher the yields, the lower the bond prices. The same applies to REITs. For some more, for some less, but, in general, it is relevant for the lion's share of the REIT players - hence, the drop in the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VNQ).

The question now is what the direction of travel will be going forward.

Could it be so that REITs will continue to remain depressed and underperform income-producing alternatives such as MLPs, dividend blue-chip corporates and various CEFs? Or is this actually an opportunity to invest heavily before the recovery takes place?

Let me discuss this in the next section.

REITs: Trap or opportunity?

I know that some of you will not be happy about this answer, but my response is that we really do not know. The reason for this is that it all depends on the systematic factors that determine the pathway for long-term yields.

Having said that, I think that it is possible though to understand which scenario is more likely than the other (i.e., REITs going up or REITs continuing to underperform).

In my humble opinion, the pessimistic scenario is more likely.

I give you three reasons for this.

First, even though REITs have experienced a decent contraction in their valuation multiples, which, theoretically, should imply an interesting opportunity, in reality, the entry point is not that attractive.

What really drives REIT valuations are the levels and its changes in the long-term yield space. This is why, for example, the recent base rate cuts have not moved the needle for REIT valuations. And if we compare REIT yields with the long-term "risk-free" yields, then the situation looks rather bad. The spread is negative. To get the spread back to the historical norm, one of the following things has to happen:

  • Long-term yields going down.
  • REIT dividend yields going up.

Second, and this is where I think the rubber hits the road. In my view, the likelihood of the long-term yields going down any time soon is extremely low. Let's take a look at what happens with the fiscal deficits, geopolitics, foreign capital outflows from the USD assets etc. These are items that are inherently supportive for having high and even rising long-term yields. So, the only realistic option that remains is REITs getting repriced lower to warrant a healthy and positive spread from the U.S. Treasury yields.

Third, we should not rule out an increasing inflation rate. The presence of notable tariffs, de-globalization, production reshoring from low-cost regions etc., are all inflationary items. Granted, so far, we have not seen material effects from the newly introduced tariffs, but we should definitely be cognizant of lagged-effects. In case the inflation level goes up, the chances are quite high that the base rates will not get reduced to, say, ~2% level. I know that a long-term yield level is what matters for REITs, but having a friendly base rate at least allows saving on interest costs through credit facilities and shorter term debt instruments. Plus, higher inflation will then make the real (not nominal) yield spread between REIT yields and the U.S. Treasury yield even less attractive for investors.

Investor action items

In a nutshell, I do not see a sector-wide bullish case for REITs any time soon.

However, it does not mean that there are no opportunities at all in this space.

Given the macro picture that I outlined above, I see the following investment plays that REIT investors could explore:

1. Real estate closed-end fund instruments: In case the base rates fall and the inflation risk does not materialize (or at least to such an extent that would keep the Fed away from cutting the rates), real estate CEF funds could deliver very strong returns. Since CEF vehicles tend to assume leverage through short-term debt instruments to magnify their overall portfolio yields, then the situation in which the base rates fall, but the asset yields remain stable or even rise could lead to an enhanced spread capture. Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred Income Fund (NYSE:RNP) is a great example with short-term debt focused leverage and exposure to relatively high-yielding and solid quality REITs.

2. Competitive cost of debt REITs: At its core, the risk of high long-term yields is about unfavorable cost of debt. It is clear that there are no mechanisms for REITs to mitigate the long-term yield risk against which their bonds/borrowings are priced. Yet, what they can do is to keep the credit risk premiums as low as possible. The way to do that is through fortress balance sheets and upper investment grade credit ratings. In this case, Realty Income Corporation (NYSE:O) is a REIT to consider (i.e., one of the few REITs with upper investment grade balance sheets), especially given how low the multiple has become, at ~P/FFO 13.1x, while the 10-year historical average is ~17.0x.

3. Screaming bargains: One can never go wrong with picking up securities that trade way below their intrinsic values. For example, in my view, this is the case for Alexandria Real Estate Equities Inc. (NYSE:ARE), which also has an upper investment grade balance sheet, ~60% FFO payout, resilient portfolio and positive lease spread statistics. However, largely due to the market fears of pharma and biotech companies reducing their R&D spend levels, ARE's stock has been completely hammered. The P/FFO has dropped to 7.6x and the dividend has jumped to 7.5% (well over the U.S. Treasury yield). Please see more details on this case in my recent ARE-specific article: Alexandria Real Estate: The True Definition Of A Dividend Bargain.

data.title: First Industrial Realty: Attractive Despite Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Shares of First Industrial Realty Trust (NYSE:FR) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining just about 3%. As a major owner of logistics real estate with a significant West Coast presence, FR has meaningful exposure to tariffs, particularly on China. That is why shares sold off sharply in early April and have not fully recovered. I last covered shares in November 2024, rating the stock a "buy;" however, initial tariff actions were harsher than I expected. As a result, the stock has lost about 8% of its value. Given this new backdrop, now is a good time to see if FR can recover or if investors should sell.

First Industrial is a leading owner of warehouse and logistic assets across the country. As you can see, it has assets across the United States, though most of its assets are located in coastal markets. That is because warehouses are disproportionately built around ports, as there needs to be a place to store imported goods after they arrive before being distributed across the country. While over the long-term this is an advantageous asset base, there are clearly near-term headwinds. I would note that given its large and diverse footprint, its top 20 tenants are only 26% of base rent.

Increased tariffs are likely to reduce imports, all else equal. Indeed, as you can see below, imports plunged in April after tariffs took effect. Of course, imports were especially high in March, as companies sought to front-run these tariffs. Given the time it takes goods to reach U.S. shores, I expect further weakness to be reported in May. However, with President Trump pulling back tariffs across nations, there will likely be some rebound in June and onwards.

The challenge is that we do not know what the ultimate tariff policy is likely to be. At this point, I assume at least a 10% baseline tariff will persist, and it seems likely that China will face higher duties than other countries, though even there the Administration has taken a more conciliatory approach. FR is highly exposed to activity near West Coast ports. Now, even if tariffs on China are higher, if that production is moved to Vietnam or India, FR should see little pressure. To the extent production is rerouted to Mexico or onshored to the U.S., it will likely be a headwind for FR, as its warehouses would no longer be near where these goods are made/arrive.

At the moment, I believe businesses are frozen. Without knowing what tariff rates will be, they are highly unlikely to make long-term commitments. Policy is so uncertain, and frequently changing, that I expect this paralysis to persist. Additionally, with tariffs implemented by executive order, the next President may simply remove them if they remain in place. If tariffs only last 3 years, we are unlikely to see a major shift to U.S. production, as it can take that long to even build a factory.

One other potential risk is that FR could see a decline in trade activity lead its customers to go bankrupt and default on their leases. It has a fairly modest $1 million set aside for bad debt expense, though even in COVID, losses were under $2 million. Importantly, FR has less than 500k square feet to China third-party logistics providers. It has largely turned down these firms for failing to meet its stringent credit requirements. It also has insignificant auto manufacturing exposure.

While policy has been surprising, at a macro level, I do not see the current tariff policy as likely to cause material changes in trade flows. Still, import volume is likely to be somewhat lower over the next twelve months, given higher prices, which could reduce consumer demand. Ultimately, I see this as a manageable headwind for the business. Unsurprisingly given how much uncertainty there is, FR is seeing conversations with tenants taking longer as customers were slowing discussions about new leases, pending the resolution of tariffs, as disclosed on the most recent earnings call.

I expect this tepid demand environment to persist for at least six months. Accordingly, management is seeing better demand for small spaces than large ones, where there is a lower commitment. Additionally, I can envision a scenario where tenants seek shorter leases. With more policy uncertainty, businesses may be comfortable with a 12 or 24 month lease, but not a 60-month lease.

While the demand side is likely weakening, there should be some offset by an improving supply side. There was a wave of construction in the wake of COVID, given very low interest rates and a surge in goods consumption. As consumption flat-lined and interest rates rose, construction of new warehouses became less economical. We are now through the worst of the completion activity, and new supply going forward should be fairly modest, at least through early 2027. Lower supply should help FR maintain occupancy and pricing power, even in a somewhat slower demand environment.

These factors can be seen in its recent financials. In the company's first quarter reported on April 16th, First Industrial generated $0.68 of FFO, which was $0.02 below expectations, even as revenue grew by 9% to $177 million. G&A rose by $4 million, a surprisingly large increase. There was nonrecurring accelerated share-based compensation, which drove this. It still expects full year G&A to be below $42 million, and costs should come back down in Q2. Adjusted EBITDA rose by 9% to $114 million, and property expenses were up just 3% from last year.

Despite the headwinds described above, FR generated 10.1% same-store net operating growth. This is a strong level, though I expect it to decelerate as the trade uncertainty drags, and year-over-year comparisons become more difficult. FR had 95.3% occupancy in Q1, down 90bps sequentially and 20bps from a year ago. Leasing activity does remain high, with new leases signed at a 30% premium from prior leases. As you can see below, the strength of the business has clearly moderated from its peak, but it continues to run at a reasonable level.

Looking forward, FR has 2 million square feet under construction, focused on the Sun Belt at a $280 million cost. So far, these projects are just 43% leased and will come online over the next nine months. It expects to have 1.5 million sq feet leased in Q4, though I see some risk it does take longer to fill this space. If uncertainty persists, I expect FR to reduce its go-forward development activity, after completing these in-the-works projects. On the positive side, FR leased the remaining 50% of its new Denver facility, pointing to ongoing demand in less trade-sensitive areas. With newbuild activity focused away from the West Coast, there should be less sensitivity to China and Asian imports.

FR also maintains a strong balance sheet. It has no maturities until 2027 on its $2.4 billion of debt, which leaves it well-insulated from interest rates. While leverage ticked up in Q1, it will come back to 4.9x as projects come online and begin contributing EBITDA, leaving leverage consistent with the low end of historic ranges.

Given this strong balance sheet, FR increased capital returns more aggressively than even I expected. In February, it raised its dividend a surprisingly strong 20% to $0.445, giving shares a 3.6% yield. At the midpoint of its guidance, shares still have a 1.6x coverage ratio. Last year, I wrote that FR could increase its dividend faster than FFO, given excess coverage, but management closed the gap more aggressively than expected. Still, FR retains over $125 million of cash flow, more than enough to meet development needs while maintaining leverage.

Guidance for $2.92 of FFO is actually higher than my $2.80 forecast from November, reflecting strong leasing at the end of last year. I do see a risk that it takes longer to lease its development pipeline, though this is unlikely to be more than a $0.05 risk. As such, I see about $2.80-$2.90 of FFO this year. Even as FFO and its dividend hike have exceeded my expectations, shares are down since November, speaking to how much negativity there is.

Frankly, I think this is an opportunity, especially as current tariff leaves are unlikely to dramatically alter trade flows, in my view. I also view a ~1.4x coverage ratio as sustainable for this business, meaning we are likely to see ongoing dividend hikes in future years. This gives shares of ~$2.00 dividend capacity, or just over a 4% yield. This dividend cushion, alongside ~5-6% underlying FFO growth, supports 6-10% dividend increases for several years to come. Combined with a starting yield of 3.6%, that points to a low double-digit total return. This makes FR an attractive buy as macro fears are outsized relative to fundamental performance.

data.title: AMDL - A Useful Speculative Tool, Not A Buy And Hold
data.description:
To grasp the construction is to grasp the use

Mankind has been building all sorts of tools for millennia now. But we do have to be careful to use the right tool for the job - both copper furnaces and frying pans are great gadgets but we do have to distinguish in their uses.

The same is true of financial instruments - they are, after all, tools designed to give certain outcomes. It helps if we understand how and why a tool is constructed so that we can then use it in the right way. Or, more importantly, don't end up using it the wrong way and so lose our hard earned money. Lose our money by simply not knowing what we're doing that is.

This particular instrument under discussion here is for speculation or hedging - not for a long tem buy and hold. So, that's the way we should use it as well.

GraniteShares 2x Long AMD Daily ETF (NASDAQ:AMDL)

What the fund intends to do is pretty much there in the title. It's from Granite, it's a leveraged - to two times - attempt to follow the long performance of AMD stock. Or, as the ETF itself puts it:

The Fund seeks daily investment results, before fees andexpenses, of 2 times (200%) the daily percentage changeof the common stock of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.(NASDAQ: AMD)

Note the daily performance there. Note also this from the fund themselves:

The Fund seeks daily leveraged investment results and are intended to be used as short-term trading vehicles. The Fund is very different from most mutual funds and exchange-traded funds.

When the guy offering for sale is insisting this is a short term trading vehicle probably wise of us to believe him.

OK, so why should we believe him?

One problem is that "daily". This leads to a daily price reset and thus:

For example, consider the example of a 3x leveraged ETF where the benchmark rises from 100 to 102 (+2%) on day-1, and falls from 102 back to 100 (-1.96%) on day-2. The benchmark index is back to its original level of 100, so has flat performance.

For the leveraged ETF, however, the return would be negative. To illustrate, after the first day of trading, an initial $100 investment on a 3x leveraged ETF would rise to $106.

$100 initial + (($100 x 2% index return) x 3) = $106

The next day the 3x leveraged ETF would drop 5.88% (3 x -1.96%), resulting in a value of only $99.77.

$106 - 5.88% = $99.77

Even though the benchmark index was flat, the leveraged ETF would have resulted in a loss of -0.23% over these 2 days.

$99.77 - $100 initial value = $-0.23

We also get the SEC making much the same point:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

The point about daily resets is compounded. For the way that the leverage is achieved that the fund buys derivatives. The point about derivatives is that they have a time value - a part of the value of a derivative is how long it lasts for. So, the passage of time, by definition, reduces the value of the derivative. This is true whatever form of derivative is used. Thus using derivatives over longer periods of time is guaranteeing a loss of value - the loss of time value of the derivative.

As the fund says:

Because of daily rebalancing and the compounding of each day’s return over time, the return of the Fund for periods longer than a singleday will be the result of each day’s returns compounded over the period, which will very likely differ from 200% of the return of theUnderlying Stock over the same period. The Fund will lose money if the Underlying Stock’s performance is flat over time,

This isn't something to hold.

Finances

This is an open ended fund so, in theory, could be subject to a redemption cascade - we all turn up asking for our money at the same time. They then have to sell the fund's assets at fire sale prices and disaster. As they're in derivatives fixing prices for a day the chance of this is so remote as to be non-existent.

As an open ended fun they're not going to stray more than mere basis points from net asset value either.

Daily volume's around the 20 million pieces level - we'll have no problem moving in and out in size. The spread's a penny - we can indeed move in size.

The management fee's 0.99%, total costs some 1.15%. Those are annual numbers, of course, so the effect of them on a - maximum - few days holding is not going to be large.

We can check this hold and lose idea.

AMDL:

AMDS:

Two times the 1x short should equal one times the 2x long. They should balance each other - and at the short end they largely do (minus a little bit in fees). Over the year there we can see that they don't, not at all. That's the long term degradation in performance given by leveraged ETFs.

So, what is AMDL for?

This is for speculation - or hedging. It's wholly possible that we might want to take a day trade on AMD stock. Doing so in a leveraged ETF gives us a cheap way of doing that - for the day, as the construction of the instrument is all about.

The other use is hedging. Say that we think - on a particular day - that AMD will outperform the S&P 500. So, we go short (probably via another ETF) the S&P and long AMD. That locks in the relative performance of AMD as against the large cap market. There are ETFs that will enable us to do this against the computing stocks, electric and so on. None of these are trading recommendations, they're simply examples.

It's impossible for me, here, to say that such speculation or hedging should be done on any particular day. Simply because it's impossible to know, ahead of time, the conditions that might make such a speculation worthwhile.

All that can be said is that AMDL is a perfectly good tool to use in such circumstances. It's designed to be so used, it's well designed to be so used. But it is to be only so used. This is a short term speculative or hedging tool, not a long term buy and hold.

Why I'm wrong

In the design and use of the tool I'm not. As to whether the tool should actually be used that's a matter of taste. Mine isn't for short term speculations but others differ.

My opinion

If we assume that the intent is short term speculation - or a hedge against just such another speculation - then AMDL is a perfectly fine instrument. But it is that, as Seeking Alpha, the SEC, Granite and I all tell you. It is not for a long term buy and hold, an investment. So, don't use it that way.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Undervalued And Oversold
data.description:
My thesis

Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) stock demonstrated price decline after Warren Buffett announced that he is retiring soon. Nevertheless, this safe harbor is still notably ahead of the broader equity market, with a 5.6% gain since early February. This means that my old bullish call aged well. I see no reason to switch to something other than Strong Buy because this stock is a great defensive play amid the current extremely uncertain environment. Despite some relief in President Trump’s trade wars, most of the trade deals with the largest partners are just temporary and recent rhetoric has not been that dovish. The valuation of Berkshire Hathaway is still quite attractive as well.

Berkshire Hathaway stock analysis

I start with the latest available quarterly financials, a crucial development for this widely diversified conglomerate. The company’s Q1 consolidated revenue of $89.7 billion was quite stable on a YoY basis, just slightly below the same quarter of FY2024. There was a notable decline in the company’s net operating earnings, though, a 14% decrease to $9.6 billion.

This was mostly explained by one-off events rather than some fundamental problems, as the insurance business saw a notable dip in underwriting income due to $1.1 billion in catastrophe losses from the California wildfires. This loss was partially offset by the improved investment income, from around $2.6 billion to $2.9 billion.

Another large segment that demonstrated earnings decline was “Manufacturing, service and retailing”. But the decline was immaterial, around $28 million for a $3 billion total. On the other hand, railroad earnings were up by a solid 6% YoY. Berkshire Hathaway Energy earnings also demonstrated strong growth, from $717 million to around $1.1 billion. As we can see, Berkshire’s operating segments demonstrate strength and ability to drive more value for shareholders. I am not mentioning the notable investment loss in Q1 because one quarter is just nothing for a company that has kept its investments for decades and has a very cautious approach to investing. The important part is that the company is in a massive $347 billion cash and cash equivalents position (including short-term securities), which can be used to invest in new promising names.

I am confident that this $347 billion cash pile will be used to create more value for shareholders because of the company’s stellar proven record of success. Warren Buffett is an investing legend who was able to beat the broader equity market for decades. Yes, he is retiring soon, but we already know the successor, Greg Abel. It is not a spontaneous decision because this succession plan was initially mentioned by Berkshire’s leadership four years ago. Therefore, Greg Abel had plenty of time to get prepared for the role. Moreover, he has been part of the conglomerate since 1999 when CalEnergy became part of Berkshire Hathaway and highly likely has been working very closely with Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger since 2018 when he joined the company’s board of directors. Therefore, his experience and the adherence to Berkshire’s values should not be a concern.

Besides company-specific positives, the environment also shows us various signs that betting on a high-quality safe harbor like Berkshire Hathaway is a reasonable move. There is still vast uncertainty related to the Fed’s next moves in terms of the monetary policy. The Fed remains quite hawkish and there were still no rate cuts this year despite less than a month left before the first half of the year ends. Higher interest rates for longer will certainly weigh on investors’ confidence in long-duration assets, making a defensive play like Berkshire Hathaway compelling.

The soaring gold price is another large sign of investors seeking safe assets. After some trade wars lull a few weeks ago, President Trump demonstrates some signs of returning to a more hawkish rhetoric with his recent words that China violated the temporary trade deal. In addition, there was an order signed regarding a large 50% tariffs increase on steel and aluminum. Therefore, the tariffs agenda does not look completely off the table.

Last but not least, the technical setup looks extremely good. The stock's current RSI indicator of around 35 is low, meaning that BRK.B is oversold. Berkshire's RSI indicator is rarely dips that low, which looks like an apparent bullish signal as well.

Intrinsic value calculation

Berkshire’s robust YTD performance helps the company to maintain position in the $1 trillion market cap club. I think it is fair that Berkshire’s valuation ratios are above the industry median because it is absolutely unparalleled in terms of the quality of management and scale. In addition, there is unmatched profitability that justifies higher multiples. The stellar track record of Warren Buffett against the S&P 500 since the 1960s is another crucial factor that makes Berkshire unique.

I believe that assessing Berkshire's valuation attractiveness through comparative analysis of its current multiples against historical ones is a sound approach. Based on this analysis, the stock is fairly valued because valuation ratios are very close to historical averages. This is very good because a company like Berkshire deserves a premium over the fair value.

Furthermore, Wall Street analysts covering Berkshire suggest that the stock is undervalued by around 6% because of the consensus target price of $521. The trend presented above suggests that the stock has been historically quite predictable in closing the gap between its actual share price and consensus target price. This reinforces the assertion that the stock is currently attractively valued.

What can go wrong with my thesis?

Investors are currently much more tolerant of taking risks than they were in March and April. This increases demand for riskier assets like growth stocks, meaning that safer harbors like Berkshire might lack attention from investors. I think that such a pivot in the stock market sentiment might be another reason (apart from the announcement that Warren Buffett retires next year) for the share price underperformance over the last month.

Another reason why Berkshire’s share price performance was weak in May is seasonality. This one has been historically the weakest month for this stock, with a mere 36.4% win rate. We also have to warn readers that June has not been historically stronger, with a slightly higher win rate of 40%. Therefore, the recent share price weakness might continue during the first month of Summer.

Summary

There are no reasons to change Berkshire Hathaway’s rating to something else other than Strong Buy. The fundamentals of this unparalleled safe harbor are rock-solid, and the stock is very attractively valued.

data.title: Stop Guessing Tomorrow: Insights On Long/Short Strategies
data.description:
By Marc Regenbaum

Mark Regenbaum shares lessons learned from two decades of experience managing long/short portfolios.

Transcript

Anu Rajakumar: In a world shaped by shifting policies, geopolitical tensions, and rapidly evolving market structures, uncertainty has become the new norm, bringing with it heightened volatility. For investors, the question isn't just how to weather the storm, but how to turn challenges into opportunities. Could long-short strategies hold the key as a powerful tool for diversification and risk management in these unpredictable times? What should investors look for in a manager as they look to capitalize on today's opportunities while mitigating risks?

My name is Anu Rajakumar, and joining me today is Marc Regenbaum, managing director and portfolio manager for the Neuberger Berman Long Short Fund. Marc, welcome to Disruptive Forces.

Marc Regenbaum: Thanks for having me, Anu.

Anu Rajakumar: Now, Marc, market environments always bring their own challenges, but the current mix of volatility and sector concentration seem particularly unique. How should investors think about navigating this environment, and what stands out to you about its current characteristics?

Marc Regenbaum: We like to say starting points matter. We believe we are living through a confluence of events today, with business cycle normalization, meaning businesses are competing more for market shares. We are also going through AI normalization, where some of that technology is maturing. We're doing all of this against a rapidly changing policy backdrop. These days, monetary, fiscal, and foreign policy are increasingly intertwined, leading to a very uneven choreography for policymakers themselves. All the while, as you said, we're sitting with markets at concentration levels rarely seen.

With that, we are seeing volatility and policy uncertainty real time in today's markets. April's market performance, starting with that downdraft and then the rapid recovery, was real-time evidence of what we're going to be experiencing going forward. That said, we do believe volatility can be seen as an opportunity rather than a risk, given the market structure. As active investors, we are suddenly more focused on the details and the risk management process we've employed for many years. For us as portfolio managers, the emphasis is on preparing for that high-volatility regime.

We like to say, think of how the markets have performed since the presidential election itself, because tariffs may come and go, but there will be continuous change, whether it's immigration policy, geopolitics, tax policy, you name it. Last but not least, you put it all together for investors. I think it's important for investors to take a perspective of whether they want to reset their portfolios, and really take a step back and reflect on how they felt during recent market swings. Did the portfolios really behave as they expected? Were they able to sleep at night? The magnitude of the change that we're seeing have just been magnificent, and you have an opportunity to adjust if you weren't really sleeping well over that period of time.

Anu Rajakumar: Yes, I'm sure the last few weeks and months indeed have been quite magnificent in a variety of different ways. Marc, I'm noticing that long-short strategies are increasingly gaining recognition. I recently saw articles about Norway's $1.8 trillion Sovereign Wealth Fund, announcing that they will be allocating to long-short managers. What does this signal about the role of these types of strategies in portfolios when it comes to diversifiers?

Marc Regenbaum: I think it's a great question, but I have to take a step back and say that one size does not fit all for long-short strategies. There are many different flavors of long-short strategies out there. Institutional firms have that due diligence capabilities to understand whether they're looking at a 130-30 fund or a market-neutral fund. There's very much different levels of risk taking through the likes of understanding gross exposures, net exposures, leverage, and concentration approaches of different long-short strategies.

For many of them, like ourselves, what's important, as you alluded to in the lead-in of the question, is, can you dampen volatility while still capture upside potential? That smoother ride phenomenon or make more by losing less. It's really important for investors to make sure they're aligning their goals and risk tolerance with the strategy that they're looking at. All of this can help decide whether long-short can be an anchor of your investment strategy, or just a broader piece of an overall asset allocation. Within the construct of today's environment, we truly believe that we are having a larger total addressable market for long short strategy opportunities, given increasing dispersion along the way.

Anu Rajakumar: Excellent. Now, Marc, I know you've been managing long-short strategies for a long time, almost two decades, I believe.

Marc Regenbaum: That's right.

Anu Rajakumar: It must have given you valuable insights over the last few years. You've gone through ups, downs, and everything in between. What lessons has history taught you about effectively managing these strategies?

Marc Regenbaum: It's a great question. One, if I was to take a step back and conduct my own due diligence on long-short managers, my checklist would really start with the following questions. First and foremost, does that manager have the ability to add value through their short process. Shorting is a very different strategy than doing your traditional long investing. Second, I'd make sure, is there a process and discipline in executing the strategies through changing environments? You want to understand how the portfolio would behave in different market environments.

Of course, where it should really shine is understanding the historical performance during the periods of volatility. How does one conduct that diligence? I think there are some specific questions you can actually go about to ask the managers. First, what is your long-term beta? That helps to give you so much information content of where it can fall within a broader portfolio asset allocation. Second, you could even use recent market environments, such as how did you perform during the challenging markets of 2022, and how did you perform during the recovery of the markets in 2023?

Remember, long-short strategies aren't just about protecting money, it's also about making money, and you have to actually be able to compound that capital back up to actually make our end clients some really attractive returns over time. It's really important to understand a manager's risk management philosophy. Look, I think if you take a step back in today's environment, one may even suggest that there's potentially clear lessons from the 2000 to 2010 market period. This is another one where you can see the opportunity of making attractive returns through not just the long side of the portfolio, but the short side of the portfolio as well. After all, from that 2000-2010 period, we actually lived through a decade that was basically a flat equity market environment.

Anu Rajakumar: Terrific. You mentioned, asking that question, what's your long-term beta? Just out of curiosity, what's reasonable when folks are looking at long-short managers, what are thresholds that they should really be thinking about?

Marc Regenbaum: Again, it goes back to what you were trying to achieve for your risk returns. I think the reality is, when you start to exceed betas that are in elevation of 0.6 or so, you're starting to be long only light, for lack of a better word. Of course, your classic market-neutral approaches would usually approach around 0 or + or -0.2. For us, we think a really nice tiptoeing to equity markets or dial-down from full-blown equity markets, said differently, your classic equity hedge, should really generally be in that, call it about 0.4 area.

Anu Rajakumar: Terrific. Thank you very much. Now, Marc, you've mentioned active management a couple of times so far, which, of course, active management plays a critical role in the success of long/short strategies. Could you talk about why active management is so important in this context?

Marc Regenbaum: Absolutely. One of my favorite topics, for sure.

Anu Rajakumar: You just lit up when I said that question.

Marc Regenbaum: I had to.

Anu Rajakumar: I love that.

Marc Regenbaum: It is the importance of knowing what you get from your active manager and, candidly, what you get from your passive management solutions as well, because passive is not riskless. It is absolutely something that is comprised of the underlying exposures that the market is made up of. When you think about today's world, we are just living in a world of volatility that has so much information content. If you are an active manager and you want to understand your artificial intelligence exposure, all you had to do is look at the market dynamics and how your portfolio performed on the day DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) entered the conversation.

If you want to understand your exposure and how much you have that's related to tariffs, understand what happened after the announcement on April 2nd, and similarly, when there was a 90-day pause just a week later. You are very much seeing dynamics and details come to life across sectors and across industries that create real opportunities for the active managers. I think in today's world, the active manager debate actually is getting a little bit watered down. I appreciate the simplicity of it, but one of the most common conversations is the Magnificent 7 versus everyone else, or the remaining 493 stocks in simplicity. It's just not how we view the world.

We think the importance of this world that we're living in today is focusing on the details. It's not as simple as just top-down versus bottom-up. There are going to be individual themes that are going to have real implications for different sectors, different companies that are going to create significant performance dispersion between winners and losers, and again, even within the same sub-sectors. Of course, the cherry on a Sundae when you think about long-short within this active conversation is some of the details that come along with that strategy. The ability to earn interest income from your short proceeds, the potential tax efficiency one can have if your strategy goes down that route.

Again, bring it all together. Long-short, I'm, of course, inherently biased by speaking about this strategy, of course. We love this environment. We are passionate about it because there's so much opportunity to take advantage of active management, to take advantage of the strategy, to make more by losing less for our underlying clients, and ultimately to bring process and risk management to today's uncertain markets.

Anu Rajakumar: Yes, absolutely. No, it's so great to see you so passionate about even tough times like these. I think a lot of investors find it very difficult to stay disciplined in these kind of uncertain environments, especially with temptations of chasing trends or reacting to volatility. Humans are emotional, and that sometimes comes through in our investment decisions.

Marc Regenbaum: For sure.

Anu Rajakumar: Marc, what advice would you give to investors on maintaining discipline during times like these?

Marc Regenbaum: You were spot on. For better or worse, I love this market backdrop. This is the type of market backdrop, I think, where process and risk management and experience comes to life. It's very different than what I like to call the FOMO markets, or the fear of missing out, which one might make the case was happening about 12 to 18 months ago. I think where I'd start the conversation, though, is when you see volatility and you see uncertainty, the first rule is stop trying to guess tomorrow.

Guessing can very much lead to investors to compounding mistakes because you can be whipsawed very quickly. Again, April was a great example of that because it would've proved very expensive if you sold at the low and then never got back in into the underlying markets themselves. Again, the importance of having a process and sticking to it during unpredictable markets or unpredictable times is paramount in today's world. After all, it's not easy.

We are absolutely inundated with information each day, and sometimes the very hardest thing to do is actually to stay the course. For us, it's about understanding process. That process itself is informed by our experience. Then, very importantly for our investors, we're hoping to use the volatility to our advantage so we can focus on their long-term goals and make attractive risk-adjusted returns throughout this uncertainty.

Anu Rajakumar: Absolutely. Uncertainty in the market doesn't just impact investors. It has broader implications for companies as well. What are some of the key challenges or adjustments that you're seeing companies face during these uncertain times?

Marc Regenbaum: I said the other was my favorite question. This might actually be something where I'll really get too excited to speak about. I love the bottom-up analysis and meeting with the companies to understand the real-world implications that we're trying to decipher as investors. The reality of the situation is that the risk management principles for companies very much parallel those that we as investors are trying to navigate, except it's probably harder. The CEOs themselves are actually the chief capital allocators. They have to focus on those balance sheets and those cash flows.

Of course, relative to investors like myself and my team, where you have liquid investment strategies, their ability to shift course is much more difficult, especially since many of their projects are very much longer lived. The challenges of today's backdrop faced by the corporations are managing supply chains, managing customer expectations, managing capital allocation in these uncertain environments. As we discussed earlier, the myriad of policy changes on the table are extremely vast, and they're significant, and that extends beyond the tariffs. The magnitude and implementation approach matters greatly, and these management teams are having to dig into the details of their own portfolios to understand how they want to adjust.

We like to say what's been going on is, as the companies are waiting to hear the rules of the actual game that we're playing today, investors are hoping for the pause that refreshes. We are seeing this real time, again, I spoke about sub-sectors, but you get very different dynamics if you are a company such as Walmart (WMT) and how they're adapting to policy changes with tariffs and their underlying mix of business, which is a little bit more consumables as opposed to discretionary products versus even their closest competitor such as Target (TGT), which has a little bit more of a product category mix that has a little bit more discretionary in terms of the nature of what their customers are buying.

Know the company's products, know their geographies, know the channels, the customer base, the pricing power of the underlying businesses. This is the stuff that active managers were trained for, that they yearn for, and now they have to actually put it to work. Combining all of that with the details of balance sheet strengths, which oftentimes equity investors overlook, can really determine how aggressive individual companies can be. Again, that can determine how much alpha generation the underlying securities can deliver as well.

Anu Rajakumar: All right. That's terrific. I think we'll end our comments there on that high note, I think, Marc. Before you go, I have to ask you one quick bonus question. I'd love to know, Marc, what is your guilty pleasure or hobby that most people wouldn't expect from you?

Marc Regenbaum: Oh, boy. That is a surprise question. I will tell you. I think what most people don't appreciate from me is, while I tend to invest in a lot of healthy foods and companies, I believe in the secular trend. I might be the unhealthiest eater that is out there. I absolutely love--

Anu Rajakumar: What's your fast food place of choice, for example?

Marc Regenbaum: Gosh. I'm a heavy chicken parm pizza and bagels kind of person. From that kind of dynamic, on the other side, I will eat in some of the-- I'll bounce it out with a little bit of Sweetgreen and McDonald's and just salad as well. I'm open to all.

Anu Rajakumar: The heart wants what the heart wants.

Marc Regenbaum: Exactly. Risk management and balance.

Anu Rajakumar: Exactly. Absolutely. Thank you for sharing that and for your insights today, Marc. In today's conversation, you really emphasized the critical role that long-short strategies can play in managing volatility and potentially capturing some upside potential as well. A few quotes that I wrote down that I really liked that you said, number one, one size does not fit all when it comes to long short strategies. You said it's important to align strategies with investor goals and risk tolerances.

Another line that you said passive is not riskless, which I think is an important reminder that we have to have. Then lastly, stop guessing tomorrow, I love that you talked about the importance of having a robust process and sticking to it, even, or I should say, especially in uncertain times as we're facing at the moment. Thank you again for being here and for sharing those thoughts today.

Marc Regenbaum: Great to be here. Thank you so much.
